title: Design a major incident war-room workflow
question: How would you implement a major incident process with coordinated communications and child incident handling?
summary: Step-by-step design for a common enterprise incident-management scenario.
scenario: The support organization wants a formal major incident process with bridge coordination, stakeholder updates, and child incident linkage.
exactAnswer: Define promotion conditions, communication ownership, child incident linking, and update cadence. The strongest answer covers workflow, visibility, and validation rather than only one business rule.
steps: Define what promotes a normal incident into major incident handling.
Add a dedicated state or flag for bridge activation and communications.
Create stakeholder communication ownership and cadence.
Expose child incident handling for duplicates and localized impact.
Validate creation, update cadence, linkage, and closure in UAT.
navigationPath: All > Service Level Management > SLA Definitions
validationChecklist: Promotion happens only under the defined condition.
Stakeholder notifications do not duplicate.
Child incidents remain linked and traceable.
commonMistakes: Treating every P1 as a major incident automatically.
Sending updates from multiple unrelated rules.

title: Prevent duplicate SLAs when incidents reopen
question: How do you prevent duplicate SLA behavior when an incident is reopened?
summary: Reopen design answer for task_sla handling and incident lifecycle.
scenario: The client reopens resolved incidents and starts seeing duplicate or inconsistent SLA behavior.
exactAnswer: First define the intended reopen behavior: resume, restart, or create a new metric. Then align SLA definitions and state transitions so only that intended behavior occurs.
steps: Document the expected business behavior for reopen.
Review start, pause, stop, and reset conditions in SLA definitions.
Confirm state transitions after resolve and close.
Adjust repair or re-evaluation logic only after the target behavior is agreed.
Retest create, resolve, close, and reopen paths.
navigationPath: All > Service Level Management > SLA Definitions
All > Service Level Management > Task SLAs
validationChecklist: Only the intended SLA stays active after reopen.
Pause and resume behavior matches expectation.
commonMistakes: Repairing SLAs before deciding the expected reopen behavior.
Ignoring update-time transitions after insert.

title: Design a standard change automation path
question: How would you implement standard change fulfillment with minimal manual approval overhead?
summary: Change-management use case covering templates, inputs, and controlled automation.
scenario: The client wants pre-approved low-risk changes to move quickly while still preserving evidence and execution tasks.
exactAnswer: Create a standard change template, validate inputs, auto-generate implementation tasks, and require closure evidence. Standard should mean pre-approved and governed, not uncontrolled.
steps: Create or review the standard change template.
Capture request inputs through a controlled form or catalog path.
Auto-generate implementation and validation tasks.
Skip ad hoc approval only for the approved standard path.
Require implementation evidence and closure notes.
validationChecklist: Only approved templates bypass ad hoc approval.
Required implementation evidence is collected.
commonMistakes: Calling a change standard without defined templates.
Removing all validation just to make it faster.

title: Prevent duplicate CIs during import and discovery overlap
question: How would you stop duplicate CI creation when imports and Discovery both update the same estate?
summary: Combines CMDB governance with integration onboarding controls.
scenario: The organization has duplicate CIs because discovery and external imports both write overlapping infrastructure data.
exactAnswer: Align every source on identification rules, assign trusted ownership through reconciliation, and route inbound updates through IRE where possible. Fix the source path before starting cleanup.
steps: Identify every source currently writing the class.
Review the identifiers each source provides.
Update integrations to use IRE or a governed path.
Define trusted ownership through reconciliation.
Clean existing duplicates only after prevention is active.
validationChecklist: New duplicates stop appearing after cutover.
Trusted fields are no longer overwritten by the wrong source.
commonMistakes: Cleaning duplicates before fixing source governance.
Allowing direct GlideRecord integrations to bypass identification logic.

title: Retire stale CIs safely
question: How would you retire stale CIs without breaking service maps or audit history?
summary: CMDB quality scenario covering controlled lifecycle changes.
scenario: The client wants to remove stale CIs, but support and audit teams still rely on historical relationships and incident records.
exactAnswer: Use a staged retirement process: identify candidates, validate ownership, retire or decommission them, review relationships, then archive or clean later if policy allows.
steps: Define stale criteria using discovery age and ownership review.
Publish candidate reports for owner validation.
Move validated records into a retired lifecycle state.
Review relationship impact before cleanup.
Archive or clean only after reporting requirements are satisfied.
validationChecklist: Owners approve retirement decisions.
Critical service relationships are reviewed before cleanup.
commonMistakes: Deleting first and asking questions later.
Retiring CIs without checking connected services or assets.

title: Design a Discovery onboarding path for a new server estate
question: How would you onboard a new Windows and Linux estate into Discovery?
summary: Covers MID placement, schedules, credentials, and validation.
scenario: A new infrastructure estate needs to be discovered without flooding the network or creating CMDB duplicates.
exactAnswer: Start with network reachability and MID capacity, define credential strategy by device class, onboard in controlled schedule waves, validate identification behavior in a test scope, then widen the estate.
steps: Confirm MID Server placement and connectivity.
Define credential groups for Windows and Linux.
Create small pilot schedules before broad rollout.
Validate CMDB identification behavior during the pilot.
Review performance and duplicates before scaling.
validationChecklist: Pilot schedules complete successfully.
Duplicate creation is not increasing.
Credential failures are understandable and low.
commonMistakes: Launching the full network range on day one.
Ignoring CMDB identification behavior until after production data is created.

title: Troubleshoot discovery credential failures
question: How would you troubleshoot a discovery schedule that suddenly started failing on credentials?
summary: A practical step-by-step Discovery troubleshooting answer.
scenario: Discovery previously worked, but now many targets fail at the credential stage.
exactAnswer: Start with MID Server health, isolate whether the failure is global or limited to one credential set or subnet, then validate password rotations, lockouts, and environment changes before altering logic.
steps: Check MID Server health and queue backlog first.
Confirm whether the failure affects all schedules or one scope.
Test the relevant credential set against representative targets.
Review recent password, domain, firewall, or privilege changes.
Adjust credential ordering only after root cause is understood.
validationChecklist: The failed schedule is isolated correctly.
The specific credential or environment change is identified.
commonMistakes: Rotating every credential at once.
Assuming discovery logic changed when the real cause is account or network drift.

title: Design a repeatable risk assessment workflow
question: How would you implement a repeatable risk assessment process in ServiceNow GRC?
summary: End-to-end design answer for risk intake, scoring, review, and treatment.
scenario: The client wants a consistent process for creating risks, scoring them, routing them to reviewers, and tracking treatment.
exactAnswer: Create a workflow that standardizes intake fields, uses a clear scoring model, assigns owners, routes high-risk items for review, and tracks treatment tasks with due dates and evidence.
steps: Define the intake model for risk statement, scope, owner, and impact.
Apply a scoring model that is transparent and reusable.
Route high-risk items to the right review group.
Create response tasks with due dates and accountability.
Track status, residual risk, and closure evidence.
validationChecklist: New risks follow the same intake pattern.
Scoring is consistent across teams.
Treatment tasks are visible and accountable.
commonMistakes: Allowing every team to score risks differently.
Closing risks without evidence of treatment or acceptance.

title: Design a policy exception approval model
question: How would you implement policy exceptions with review, expiry, and evidence?
summary: Governance scenario for controlled risk acceptance.
scenario: The client allows temporary exceptions to policy but wants them governed and reviewable.
exactAnswer: Capture the exception request, reason, scope, compensating controls, owner, and end date. Route it for approval, create renewal reminders, and prevent indefinite exceptions without review.
steps: Define required exception data and evidence.
Route exceptions to the right authority.
Set expiry dates and reminder cadence.
Create follow-up tasks before expiry.
Report on active, expiring, and overdue exceptions.
validationChecklist: Exceptions always have owner, reason, and end date.
Expiring exceptions trigger review before they lapse.
commonMistakes: Allowing exceptions with no end date.
Treating a policy exception as a replacement for remediation planning.

title: Design vulnerability response assignment and SLA model
question: How would you route vulnerable items to the right remediation teams with meaningful SLAs?
summary: Core SecOps implementation scenario focused on ownership and timing.
scenario: The client wants vulnerable items to reach the correct resolver groups with due dates tied to severity and business criticality.
exactAnswer: Define routing inputs such as owning team, CI, service, asset criticality, and vulnerability severity. Then implement assignment logic, SLA policies, exception handling, and dashboards.
steps: Confirm which fields determine ownership and priority.
Map vulnerable items to remediation teams using CI or service ownership.
Define due-date behavior by severity and criticality.
Create exception and false-positive paths.
Report on overdue backlog and ownership quality.
validationChecklist: High severity items route correctly.
SLA behavior matches policy.
commonMistakes: Routing only by scanner source.
Ignoring CI or service ownership when assigning remediation.

title: Design a security incident triage flow
question: How would you implement triage steps for inbound security incidents before investigation?
summary: SecOps scenario for consistent early-stage handling.
scenario: Security incidents arrive from multiple alert sources and analysts need a consistent triage process.
exactAnswer: Implement a triage stage that captures scope, severity, duplicate checks, asset context, and whether containment is needed. Then hand off validated cases into deeper investigation.
steps: Define the triage checklist and minimum required context.
Add enrichment steps for asset or user context.
Check for duplicate active cases.
Escalate only after severity and scope are understood.
Capture triage outcome clearly.
validationChecklist: Analysts follow the same early-stage checklist.
Duplicate cases are reduced.
Investigators receive enough context to continue.
commonMistakes: Jumping into full investigation on every noisy alert.
Skipping business context during triage.

title: Design hardware lifecycle from stock to disposal
question: How would you implement a controlled hardware lifecycle from receipt through disposal?
summary: HAM scenario covering custody, assignment, and retirement.
scenario: The client wants clear hardware stages from receiving through stock, deployment, reassignment, and disposal.
exactAnswer: Define lifecycle states, custody updates, stockroom handling, assigned-user changes, and disposal evidence so operational moves and audit history stay synchronized.
steps: Define lifecycle states such as in stock, in use, in repair, retired, and disposed.
Capture receipt into the correct stockroom.
Update assignment and custody during deployment and reassignment.
Require approval and evidence for disposal.
Report on aging, stock, and retired asset quality.
validationChecklist: State changes are controlled.
Stockroom and assignee fields stay aligned to actual custody.
Disposal evidence is retained.
commonMistakes: Using one generic status for every stage.
Retiring hardware without recording disposition details.

title: Design a software reclamation campaign
question: How would you implement a reclamation campaign for unused software installs?
summary: SAM optimization scenario focused on measurable savings.
scenario: The organization wants to reduce waste from inactive software installations without frustrating business users.
exactAnswer: Use usage data and thresholds to identify candidates, send controlled notices, allow opt-out where justified, then remove or reassign licenses and track realized savings.
steps: Define inactivity thresholds and exclusion rules.
Generate candidate lists and business-owner reviews.
Send user or manager notices with response options.
Remove or reclaim only after the review window.
Track reclaimed licenses and cost impact.
validationChecklist: Candidates reflect genuine inactivity.
Exclusions are honored.
Savings and reclaimed counts are measurable.
commonMistakes: Auto-removing software without review for critical roles.
Using weak normalization and misidentifying products.

title: Design asset-to-CI sync and ownership alignment
question: How would you keep hardware assets and configuration items aligned over time?
scenario: Support teams rely on CI relationships while asset teams rely on financial and custody data, and the records drift apart.
exactAnswer: Define when an asset should create or link to a CI, which fields should synchronize, and how reassignment, retirement, and disposal update both sides without duplicating ownership logic.
steps: Identify which asset classes must map to CIs.
Define the synchronization points and source of truth for key fields.
Handle reassignment, retirement, and disposal explicitly.
Report on mismatches so drift becomes visible.
validationChecklist: Critical asset classes have matching CIs where expected.
Ownership drift is reportable.
Retirement updates both lifecycle views correctly.
commonMistakes: Trying to synchronize every field in both directions.
Ignoring retire and dispose events in the sync design.
